title: Longest Consecutive Numbers in an Array

content.problemStatement:
You are given an array numsnumsnums containing integers.

Your task is to find the **size of the largest subset of consecutive integers**.
The order of elements in the original array does not matter — they can be rearranged to form the consecutive sequence.
The test cases may contain duplicate values.

Function DeclarationFunction NamelongestConsecutivelongestConsecutivelongestConsecutive – This function returns the length of the longest sequence of consecutive integers in the array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : An array of integers (may contain duplicates and negative values).
Return Value
Returns a single integer — the **length of the longest consecutive integer sequence**.
Constraints
1≤nums.length≤1051 \leq nums.length \leq 10^51≤nums.length≤105
−109≤nums[i]≤109−10^9 \leq nums[i] \leq 10^9−109≤nums[i]≤109
Duplicate values may exist

Input Format


The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.




Each test case consists of multiple lines of input:



The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of elements in the array.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers nums[i]nums[i]nums[i] — the elements of the array.




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the size of the longest consecutive sequence.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the length of the longest consecutive integer sequence that can be formed from the given numbers (duplicates are ignored).

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: numbers 12‑13‑14‑15‑16 form a run of length 5. Second test case: 1‑2‑3‑4 is the longest run (length 4). Third test case: 100‑101‑102‑103 is the longest run (length 4).
Negative numbers -6 to -1 are consecutive (length 6). After removing duplicates, the second array contains 1‑6 consecutively (length 6).
A single element always yields length 1. In the second case no two numbers are consecutive, so the maximum length is also 1.

solution.approach: For each test case we insert all numbers into a hash set to eliminate duplicates and allow O(1) look‑ups. Then we iterate over the set; a number x is the start of a consecutive run if x‑1 is not in the set. From each start we extend forward while x+1, x+2, … are present, counting the length of that run. The maximum length over all runs is the answer. This runs in linear time on the number of distinct elements and uses linear extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of elements (duplicates are ignored). Overall O(T·N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the hash set storing distinct numbers.
